reasoning: The rule is presented as explicitly prohibiting safety interventions ('even in safety-critical situations'), which mandates negligence in the face of immediate physical danger to bystanders. No reasonable person would defend a policy that prioritizes jurisdictional boundaries over preventing a steel beam from falling on a sidewalk.

The rule imposes a regressive flat tax on the lowest-paid members to fund a luxury facility they are explicitly banned from using. This mirrors the 'pool assessment' example in the guidelines; no reasonable person would defend extracting significant wealth from struggling apprentices to subsidize a lounge for high-earning seniors that the apprentices cannot enter.

The rule mandates an action that is physically impossible to perform (faxing a disconnected number) while imposing severe professional penalties for non-compliance. No reasonable person would defend a policy that punishes individuals for failing to use infrastructure that the authority itself has dismantled.

The rule mandates gross inefficiency and causes physical injury (RSI) solely to artificially inflate costs, which a reasonable person would view as exploitative rather than a legitimate labor protection. While work preservation is a known concept, banning standard power tools for high-volume drywall work at the expense of worker health crosses the line into obvious absurdity.

The rule mandates compliance that is physically impossible (collecting a ballot from a condemned, boarded-up building). No reasonable person would defend a voting procedure that requires entering a closed, hazardous location without providing an alternative.

Compelling members to harass a manager's wife and children at their private home as a condition of maintaining health insurance is morally repugnant. No reasonable person would defend a mandate to 'scream at kids' as a legitimate or just organizational policy.

The rule enforces a direct wealth transfer where one group pays a specific surcharge to fund a benefit they are explicitly barred from receiving. This mirrors the passing example of residents paying for a pool they cannot access; it is structurally predatory and lacks reciprocal benefit.

The rule enforces a significant financial penalty ($400 staffing fee) for a service that is factually obsolete and unnecessary (maintaining a laptop like a 1960s organ). A reasonable person would immediately recognize paying a technician to watch a laptop as an unjust, rent-seeking enforcement of an outdated regulation.

The policy mandates an action (uploading to a specific portal) that is factually impossible because the infrastructure has been decommissioned. Fining members for failing to perform an impossible task due to the authority's own technical failure is absurd and indefensible to any reasonable observer.

The rule is rendered absurd by the structural fact that the mandated alternative (the transfer truck) only runs once a week, forcing workers to sit idle for days. While jurisdictional rules can be defended in the abstract, this specific implementation creates obvious waste and inefficiency that no reasonable person would justify.
